text: How to make the current ATM even "Smarter" I've been thinking about this for a while, but yesterday’s RNS reinforced how responsive and nimble the SWC team is and they’re clearly open to feedback, which is great. I know they are in the constant process of evaluating the structure so this is just my feedback which may already have been considered in some format. TL;DR: The current ATM design feels too static. It should be more dynamic, designed to respond to real time volatility and price action, especially when the stock trades at elevated mNAV multiples. That flexibility could allow the company to capitalise on price spikes and reduce dependence on perfect daily timing. Volatility = Opportunity By my calculations, SWC’s 30-day realized volatility is over 300%, meaning it moves on average ~18% per day (absolute terms) making it the highest vol stock in the UK. As we know, "volatility is vitality" and this kind of price action is a feature, not a bug, but only if the ATM is structured to capture it. The current ATM framework Right now, the ATM has two main rules: 1) No selling below the previous day's close, 2) Capped at 20% volume. These constraints made sense initially, a cautious approach that avoids missteps in execution and incorrect FUD that $MSTR often saw (ATM is compounding pressure on down days). But with the current volatility, what we've learned since it's launch and the evolving liquidity profile, this framework feels a little too rigid. It misses opportunities to issue into temporary over-extensions where the stock trades at significantly elevated mNAV multiples, even if just for a few hours or a single day. There is a lot of scope to amend it and make it super constructive. Let's walk through an example Say $SWC closes at 250p. The next day, due to broad market news (e.g. Fed cut interest rates more than expected), the stock jumps to 350p (+40%), trading well above recent mNAV levels. Under current rules, the company can sell into this strength. But if the following days assume the market can't hold the gains and the stock retraces 10% to 315p, then drops another 10% the the next day to 283p, and then 255p the next day, we’ve been unable to sell, even though the stock was still trading above where it was before the rally. Why? Because we’ve hard-coded a rule that says, "only sell above the previous day's close." That means we've tied our own hands and missed the opportunistic window to issue equity at highly accretive levels. One of the best trading lessons I ever got was: “Trade when you can, not when you have to.” There will be moments when real liquidity appears, either on the order book or OTC, and Shard should have the flexibility to act. Taking the example above, if the stock closed at 350p and the next day opened with a 345/355 spread and we had a buyer bidding for a large block at 345 (bid side), the current rules would still prevent execution. That’s a missed opportunity. What's the solution? A more dynamic matrix that allows for flexibility A dynamic matrix that evaluates net price movements rather than just previous day closes. Example, If the stock was +30% the day before, maybe we can still sell even if it's -10% the next day (still +20% net). Or +10% yesterday, -5% today = still +5% overall. Consider mNAV multiples as the gating factor, not just price. For example: "Can sell so long as mNAV > 3x", regardless of day to day price shifts. Possibly allow limited volume issuance on down days if stock remains at attractive mNAVs. E.g., allow 5–10% issuance on lower days if accretion is high. Or there could be rules around the volume cap that allow us to be flexible around the 20% cap. E.g for OTC trades, there is no volume cap - taking the example above, if the closing price was 350p and we had an opportunity to do a 5m share block the next day at the open at 345p (based off a 345/355 spread), we should be allowed to capture that. Why this matters now? Right now, $SWC is in the critical stage of getting from 1000 to 10,000 BTC and reaching a level where the balance sheet is strong enough to issue other capital market instruments (e.g preferreds) that can take pressure off an ATM facility. Moreover, the stock is trading at ~3.5x mNAV, which is still extremely accretive. While increased flexibility might temporarily cap the share price, the ATM is our primary weapon and using it aggressively is part of the "growing pains" needed to get enough bitcoin at this crucial stage. Remember, the more Bitcoin we have on balance sheet, the more sustainable this path becomes towards reaching key milestones. As such, given the stock’s volatility, a more dynamic approach makes sense. Smarter Web have done an amazing job to date and shown they’re open to feedback given how quickly market conditions are changing and what we're learning each day. Moreover, this is definitely something they could explore with Shard and Tennyson, who are also incentivised to optimise execution and innovate around structure given the fees they receive. Happy to hear feedback, Jamie @asjwebley @Croesus_BTC

text: Longer post below. TL;DR: August may be quieter due to seasonality but progress continues, stay focused on the bigger picture. As we head into August, I want to reiterate something important that will shape sentiment and community energy over the coming weeks. August is historically (if not the) quietest month across global markets, especially equities and capital markets. Earnings season is over, the macro calendar thins out, trading volumes can drop by 20–40%, and institutional activity slows down as portfolio managers, traders, and bankers take time off. It’s a well documented seasonal pause, often referred to as the “dead month” for good reason. I speak from personal experience having spent many summers working on trading floors witnessing this. Why does this matter? Because we’ve become accustomed to a steady rhythm of RNS announcements, sometimes multiple per week, and if that pace slows during August, it shouldn’t be seen as a sign that nothing is happening behind the scenes. Progress is ongoing and will continue (AW already confirmed this), but it may move at a slightly slower pace, not due to any slowdown on the Smarter Web side, but rather with the external parties we’re engaging with. This also applies to capital raising and the three funding mechanisms we have in place (ATM, ABBs, Direct Subs) and how it impacts the timing of the next Bitcoin purchase over the month of August. On the current 14m share tranche of the ATM-like facility, community estimates suggest we’re around 50% complete. It’s been a tough couple of weeks for all BTCTCs, but it feels like $SWC and $TSWCF have found a local bottom. With mNAV still around 3.7x, all sells remain highly accretive. That said, given the lower equity volumes in August, it could take Shard slightly longer to complete. The cash will come, it may just take time, but when it does, it should be enough to facilitate our largest Bitcoin purchase to date. Regarding ABBs, in my experience, August tends to be a very quiet month as institutional appetite to allocate capital or take on risk is generally lower, though not absent. That said, it’s worth highlighting that the last raise of ~£17m was, in my view, a big success given the market conditions at the time. It demonstrated the team’s tactical agility - few could have executed a raise of that size under the market circumstances (share price and sentiment across all BTCTCs had been rolling over). It’s important to consider not just the amount raised, but the context in which it was achieved. Direct subscriptions remain a viable option, particularly if a key price level is reached and a motivated buyer steps in. It’s the broker’s role to stay close to those conversations and keep the dialogue active. Since IPO, we’ve seen regular buys, but it’s important to remember that during Metaplanet’s accumulation phase from 1,000 to 2,100 BTC (Oct 2024 to Feb 2025), there were stretches of 3 to 8 weeks between purchases. That cadence is completely normal. If we don’t see a buy for a few weeks, that’s okay, think slightly longer term. The progress of over the last three months has been exponential, as covered in Friday’s post. But it won’t be a straight line up and we need to be realistic about the seasonal slowdown. That’s where the community comes in: to keep the energy high, engagement strong, and to show why what we’re building is fundamentally different. More to come soon. Onwards and upwards, together.

text: Summer Seasonality and Equity Volumes: Context Matters A reminder that July and August are typically the quietest months for global equities. Average trading volumes during this period are often 20–40% lower, driven largely by summer seasonality which is a well documented and long observed trend across investment banks/brokerages. Activity usually picks up again in September, once people return from holidays and desks are fully staffed. So when reviewing trading volumes, don’t mistake lower headline numbers for a lack of interest in a stock. More often than not, it’s simply a reflection of the seasonal slowdown.

text: Direct Subscriptions: A Third Capital Raising Route for Smarter Web It’s worth remembering that Smarter Web has a third way of raising capital via Direct Subscription Agreements, alongside ABBs and the ATM-like facility. The company has already completed two such strategic raises: July 9th: £10.3m raised at 325p June 23rd: £3.8m raised at 495p These are not ABBs but bespoke private placements with a single strategic investor, structured and executed directly with the company - there are normally two scenarios. 1. Investor approaches the company (unsolicited) A hedge fund, family office, or HNW investor sees value in SWC. They reach out directly to the company or its broker to express interest in a block investment at a particular price level. If SWC agrees, they arrange a direct subscription, issuing new shares directly to the investor (has so far been done at the closing price) and issue a subsequent RNS. 2. Company cultivates investor demand over time SWC meets investors through roadshows, broker introductions or ongoing dialogue. As the company updates its story an investor may become interested in deploying capital. When the investor sees a compelling price level, they make a subscription proposal. If terms are right and SWC agree, SWC issues new shares directly in a bespoke, negotiated transaction. Why this matters? This is an important additional capital-raising tool beyond traditional bookbuilds or ATM-like mechanisms: Enables fast, strategic funding without market disruption or relying on "price being up", reflects strong investor conviction in the company and shows that SWC is actively engaging capital partners behind the scenes. While not always visible day to day, we must trust that Andrew and the team are working in the background to cultivate and convert these types of opportunities, ready to act when the price and partner align. * Views on process are my own and based off my own experience. All feedback welcome.

text: Hi Andrew, Thanks for the update and congratulations on another fantastic week. To borrow a baseball analogy: it’s like advancing base by base instead of swinging for the fences and this builds credibility. text: Bullish research report from Tennyson Securities $SWC has raised £17.5m in an at-market equity raise at 295 pence per share. Prior to the raise, SWC held approximately £147 million in treasury: 1,600 BTC and £4.0 million in cash. The raise adds ~£21 million in cash for Bitcoin purchases in the coming days. This figure may increase further depending on the outcome of its ATM-like facility. SWC is expected to surpass 2,000 BTC shortly, becoming the 22nd largest public holder of Bitcoin globally. The equity raise is highly accretive to shareholders due to the premium to NAV. Assuming full BTC deployment at current prices, mNAV improves from 5.0x to 4.5x. Share count rises by just 2%, increasing BTC per share by 9%, from 667 to 725 sats per share. This marks a >300x increase in sats per share since the company’s April IPO. SWC has generated a BTC yield of 7.4% per day over the last 85 days. Days to mNAV cover now stands at just 21 days—significantly faster than peers like Metaplanet (3350 JP), The Blockchain Group (ALTBG FP), and MicroStrategy (MSTR US). Rapid growth is attracting global investor interest and boosting SWC’s international reputation. SWC’s competitive edge is supported by: First-mover advantage in UK capital markets.l, Strong trust in the leadership team, Political and cultural resistance to BTC in the UK, which deters larger incumbents (e.g. FTSE350 firms), Growing international investor support. SWC remains the most actively traded stock on the Aquis Exchange, averaging £6.7m in daily volume since IPO. Recent daily trading volume has exceeded £20m. US OTC listing in June significantly boosted volume—over 60% of trades now come from US investors. While mNAV compression is expected over time in Bitcoin Treasury Companies, SWC’s ability to maintain high daily BTC yield justifies a higher near-term mNAV premium versus global peers.

text: Open letter to @asjwebley to say congratulations and thank you Hi Andrew, I wanted to say thank you for taking the time to speak this afternoon on the live call with @ourgoodlifeuk . As always, it was an insightful conversation, and I really appreciate your willingness to engage so openly. You continue to raise the bar in terms of transparency and engagement - no other UK CEO's of public listed companies are doing what you are. Most importantly, congratulations on another fantastic week. You've achieved some huge milestones this week in advancing the Bitcoin stack, and it’s unfortunate that the news around UTXO/SATs and the noise that followed drew attention away from that. Despite the distractions, your leadership continues to stand out. I can only imagine how challenging the past week must have been, dealing with this from multiple angles and taking time away from the "core mission". You’ve handled everything with a lot of grace and composure which I know isn’t easy. If I were in your position, I would’ve also been shocked by how the news surfaced. Having worked in investment banking, I understand how sub-optimal communication can be, and hearing important updates third hand, as you did, is far from ideal. I completely agree there’s an inherent conflict of interest, and it’s surprising that it wasn't recognized earlier. That said, we’re all human, and mistakes happen. You’ve taken the high road via choosing to move forward and focus on what matters: continuing to build Smarter Web responsibly and successfully. That’s leadership. I’ve also noticed how present you've been on X, responding thoughtfully and transparently to the community. It's time consuming and, I’m sure, at times frustrating especially when you’re addressing the same questions repeatedly or dealing with wild speculation. Still, you’ve shown incredible patience and professionalism. You're doing an amazing job, Andrew. And while I know you don’t need me to tell you that, I hope it’s a reminder that many of us see and appreciate your work and the way you lead. As Smarter Web continues to grow, new challenges will inevitably arise but I believe those are also opportunities for your leadership to shine in new ways. In fact, the way you've handled this week I'm sure will further enhance the community’s trust in you. You’ve built a strong team, with @Croesus_BTC and others by your side, and you have a community that’s here to support you every step of the way. I hope you get some time to rest this weekend, touch some grass, recharge and then come back refreshed for another big week. And for what it’s worth, the orange shirt looked fantastic - it really is your colour. Jamie @tylev @DavidFBailey

text: FTSE Aspirations & Largest UK Company Vision Andrew reiterated his desire for Smarter Webs to be FTSE listed one day, something I personally see as a question of “when, not if” — an operational logistics exercise. The long-term ambition? To become the largest public company in the UK. It may sound bold, but consider: •210,000 BTC (1% of supply) •At a conservative 2.5x mNAV multiple •Requires Bitcoin at $410,000 for Smarter Web to surpass AstraZeneca’s £160 billion market cap If they accumulate 20,000 BTC per year, that’s achievable within a decade. The vision is clear — and it’s refreshing to hear a UK-listed CEO with such ambition. ⸻ Strategic Acquisitions & Financing Strategy Smarter Web has plans to grow via strategic acquisitions, though it’s unclear yet what this would look like. MicroStrategy has historically avoided acquisitions, favouring pure Bitcoin exposure as the hurdle Rate is high to bear. It will be interesting to see how Smarter Web balances this. For now, equity remains the preferred financing tool. •Saylor and Andrew discussed the pros and cons of convertibles •Other instruments remain on the table, but aren’t currently the right fit for now •The ATM-style facility is being tested, and I think there will be a focus on data and process improvement ⸻ Transparency, Premium, and Market Perception Andrew reiterated Smarter Web’s commitment to fairness and transparency, notably: •No discounted placings — a stark contrast to other BTC investment vehicles. Just look at how they are constantly raising the bar - Weekly updates and a public analytics dashboard showing cash position available for BTC purchases etc. The market agrees: when asked what matters most in a BTCTC, the audience ranked “trust, management credibility, and execution” top. Constructive feedback? Smarter Web needs crisp, varied responses to two key questions: 1.Why buy Smarter Web over Bitcoin? 2.Why pay a 5x premium for Smarter Web shares? It’s worth having a variety of answers depending on the audience and format (retail vs insto, quick TV interview where I have 20/30 seconds vs a tricky host or 5mins in a closed door meeting with an open investor). A simple explanation around increasing satoshis per share through capital market activity will help both retail and institutional audiences. ⸻ Retail Market Power & Liquidity The Winterfloods MD shared eye-opening stats: •UK retail accounts for just 10% of volume (vs. 30% in the US) •Smarter Web is leading a shift — trading more volume than many FTSE names •It’s consistently among the most bought stocks on platforms like HL This support community data which shows Shares traded as a % of total float ranks near the top of all UK equities The momentum is undeniable. ⸻ Final Reflections The buzz around Smarter Web is real. After the presentation, Andrew was mobbed — taking photos, shaking hands, hearing personal stories from investors. He clearly values the community — visible in both his public presence and how he interacted yesterday. But make no mistake, we are still early. Many institutions and retail investors are only now discovering Smarter Web or sitting on the sidelines watching. For me, Smarter Web is the most exciting story in UK equities. It’s poised to be the dominant name in this space. Not financial advice, but I fully expect Smarter Web to chase — and potentially claim — the title of fastest 1,000x performer (£25 target) from IPO. See excellent scenario analysis from @vostra and others for more on that. Each week: •Cash reserves grow •Strategy refines •Awareness compounds •Intelligence gathers What excites me most? There are likely strategies and opportunities Smarter Web hasn’t even considered yet but will, as they scale. The community is galvanised. Contributions from all angles — data tools, liquidity trackers, global perspectives are all making us setting us apart and “₿e Smarter”

text: Smarter Web Presentation – Peterhouse Capital Event: Key Takeaways Note: long post, all thoughts and interpretations are my own. Couldn’t make the event? Don’t worry, I’ve got you covered with all the key insights. Grab a brew, settle in — this is a longer post but worth your time (note it’s a thread). ⸻ Atmosphere & Interest The turnout exceeded expectations. Peterhouse mentioned applications were double what they anticipated —a healthy mix of retail and institutions. While the agenda featured multiple panels, almost everyone I spoke with was there for one reason: Smarter Web. During Andrew’s presentation, the room was standing-room only — easily 200 to 250 people packed in. ⸻ Andrew’s Delivery & Progress Andrew delivered a focused, positive, 15-minute update with his trademark humility and quiet confidence. If you’ve followed him for a while, it’s clear he’s evolving — his delivery and presence are sharpening. Speaking to 200+ people isn’t easy, but I expect him to be addressing 1,000+ audiences with ease in the near future, much like Saylor developed his own style. Andrew’s calm, grounded charisma continues to resonate. People buy into him — and by extension Smarter Web — not just because of the vision, but because he’s executing and hitting milestones. ⸻ Business Foundation: The Operating Business A major emphasis was placed on the company’s 15-year operating track record: •250+ clients •Profitable, cash-generative model •Adopted a Bitcoin treasury strategy as a deliberate, long-term store of value This distinction is critical. Smarter Web isn’t an opportunistic Bitcoin shell — they’re an established business that pivoted towards Bitcoin, which arguably provides more regulatory comfort. That sentiment was reinforced by the regulatory panel earlier in the day. ⸻ Milestones & Aspirations The crowd loved hearing stats — particularly Smarter Web achieving a 100x valuation in just 51 days, the fastest ever for a global company. For context: •ASOS holds the UK IPO-to-peak multiple record with a 385x return (20p IPO in 2001 to £77 in 2018) •For Smarter Web to beat that? They need to hit £9.62 — to me, matter of when, not if. ⸻ Insights from the Saylor meeting Andrew shared some details from conversations with Michael Saylor and Phong Lee (CEO of MicroStrategy) regarding the longer-term potential to issue preferred securities — purely conceptual at this stage. Saylor even joked he’d be happy to let them use similar naming conventions (“Smarter Strike,” “Smarter Strife”) and offered to provide all materials related on how to do it. The UK fixed-income market is vast yet yield-starved — done right, this could be significant. Regulatory hurdles would be a hurdle (who knows how big), but with Japan’s Metaplanet exploring similar models, it’s a space to watch. The key differentiator? Smarter Web has direct access to Saylor’s playbook and his ongoing support — no other UK-listed Bitcoin company can say that. Combine that with UTXO intelligence and Jesse Myers (Andrew’s emerging right-hand man), and the strategic network surrounding Smarter Web keeps strengthening. ⸻ The Ambition: 1% of All Bitcoin The boldest takeaway? Smarter Web’s ambition to accumulate 1% of the entire Bitcoin supply. It sounds big, but: •They’ve already smashed near-term targets (soon 1,000 BTC and expect 2,100 BTC) •The capital markets “flywheel” via book builds, ATM-style facilities, etc. continues to strengthen •Andrew’s tone is cautiously optimistic, grounded in detailed scenario analysis Think back: $1m Bitcoin once sounded impossible. But every milestone ($10, $100, $1,000, $10,000, $100,000) has made the next step more plausible. Smarter Web’s incremental, measurable goals — 10,000 BTC, 21,000 BTC, etc. — build credibility along the way.

The European listing was a key component of this strategy, aimed at boosting liquidity "which is what it's all about". Expanding access in Asia is on Andrew's agenda. Community Engagement: Andrew expressed his appreciation for the many messages received from shareholders, sharing how the investment has positively impacted their lives — a point he finds touching. Other Updates: A small teaser regarding upcoming changes to the website — more details to follow. Andrew will be attending the Peterhouse event in London tomorrow.

text: I also took your chart and put it into chat-gpt to give some feedback which it did below (apols for the long text but some good points which may be helpful) 1. Legend Placement & Clarity Move the legend below or to the right of the chart to avoid overlapping with the bars. Consider using more distinct colors (especially between EU and Total Volume) to avoid visual confusion. 2. Color Differentiation Use contrasting colors that are colorblind-friendly. Suggestion: UK Volume: Blue USA Volume: Orange EU Volume: Green Total Volume: Dark Red or Black for clear distinction 3. Label Readability Rotate x-axis labels slightly (45°) to prevent overlap, especially on tightly packed dates. Increase font size of axis labels and data labels for easier reading. 4. Data Labels Optimization Only show data labels for the "Total Volume" bars to reduce clutter. Alternatively, only show labels for significant peaks or threshold-exceeding volumes. 5. Gridlines & Axis Formatting Add horizontal gridlines to improve readability of volume heights. Use consistent and precise tick marks on the y-axis (e.g., 2M, 4M, 6M, etc.). Optionally format volume numbers with commas for clarity (e.g., 1,000,000 vs. 1M). 6. Grouping & Bar Width Group bars more tightly or use stacked bar charts if comparisons between regions aren't crucial individually.
